topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email arrived at three in the morning, a curt notification about the departmental restructuring and Dr. Alistair’s immediate departure. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead reflecting dully in my eyes. There was no fanfare, no lingering sentiment, just a formal notification of his retirement. I picked up my lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic cool against my palm, and took a slow sip. It tasted like ash. I didn't feel a sharp pang of loss, just a flat, heavy sense of finality settling in my chest. I put the mug down without ceremony, the clink against the desk sounding strangely loud in the silence of my small office. I began sorting through the files on my desk, the motions automatic, devoid of any real urgency or sadness. He was gone. It was just a change in the landscape, nothing more.

[story 2]

When Sarah told me, leaning against the water cooler, that Professor Davies was stepping down, I nodded slowly. My gaze drifted to the reflection in the glass partition of the hallway; I saw only my own face, tired and unengaged. I offered a brief, practiced smile. "That's… news," I managed, the words feeling thin and unenthusiastic. I watched her chatter on about his decades of contributions, the warmth in her voice seeming excessive to me. I simply shifted my weight, crossing one ankle over the other, and let the silence stretch between us. There was no immediate desire to inquire further, no urge to offer congratulations. The situation simply registered as an administrative footnote, a closing chapter that hadn't demanded my attention in the first place.

[story 3]

I found the notice tucked into the corner of the staff bulletin board during my routine morning sweep. Alistair. Retired. The words swam briefly before settling into a dull, unremarkable shape. I walked back to my workstation, my steps measured and even. I didn't pause to reread the text, nor did I allow my expression to contort. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, hovering for a moment before landing on an unrelated spreadsheet. The air in the room felt stale, the usual hum of activity seeming distant. I leaned back in my chair, letting my shoulders drop slightly, a physical manifestation of a release I wasn't sure I was seeking. The news was processed, filed away, and then I moved on to the next task, the world continuing its predictable rotation around me, entirely unperturbed by the absence of that one figure.
